findings.title: Optimizer loop has no timeout or cancellation path
findings.why_it_matters: A 200-combo run with slow yfinance fetches (cold cache, intraday 1-min data) can take 30-90 seconds. The synchronous route occupies a uvicorn threadpool slot for the entire duration. If the client disconnects mid-run, the server still executes all remaining backtests with no way to abort. Under repeated optimizer submissions the threadpool can saturate, blocking all other synchronous routes (bot API, account bar) until slots drain.
findings.suggested_fix: Convert to async def and check request.is_disconnected() between iterations. Alternatively add a hard wall-clock budget via concurrent.futures with a 60s total timeout.
evidence: Line 59: def optimize_backtest(req: OptimizeRequest) — synchronous def, runs in threadpool
Line 87: for combo in product(*[p.values for p in req.params]): — tight loop with no yield or cancellation check between iterations
start.sh launches uvicorn with default 1 worker and no --workers flag; threadpool saturation by CPU-bound backtests reduces effective concurrency for all synchronous routes
_MAX_COMBOS = 200 and run_backtest() calls _fetch() which hits network on cache miss

findings.title: useAccountQuery missing adaptive interval on broker unhealthy
findings.why_it_matters: When any broker is unhealthy, the 4 other polling hooks back off to 10s via adaptiveMs(). useAccountQuery uses a fixed 30s interval and never reads broker health, leaving the adaptive-interval system incomplete.
findings.suggested_fix: Add const qc = useQueryClient() and change refetchInterval: 30_000 to refetchInterval: () => adaptiveMs(qc, 30_000).
evidence: Lines 36-44: useAccountQuery uses refetchInterval: 30_000 (fixed constant, no qc declared)
Lines 9-12: adaptiveMs() helper returns Math.max(normalMs, 10_000) when any broker unhealthy
useBotsQuery (line 9), useJournalQuery (line 21), usePositionsQuery (line 32) all pass through adaptiveMs

findings.title: useOrdersQuery also missing adaptive interval
findings.why_it_matters: Same inconsistency as useAccountQuery: orders poll at fixed 30s regardless of broker health. Lower severity because orders are less time-sensitive.
findings.suggested_fix: Add const qc = useQueryClient() and change refetchInterval: 30_000 to refetchInterval: () => adaptiveMs(qc, 30_000).
evidence: Lines 54-60: useOrdersQuery uses refetchInterval: 30_000 (fixed constant, no qc declared)
No qc parameter declared in this hook, unlike useBotsQuery/useJournalQuery/usePositionsQuery

findings.title: Optimizer silently skips 500 errors as if they were 400 validation skips
findings.why_it_matters: run_backtest wraps all its internal exceptions as HTTPException(500). The catch block conflates 400-type invalid-param skips with 500-type engine failures: a systematic bug affecting all 200 combos returns results=[] skipped=200 with no diagnostic detail.
findings.suggested_fix: Re-raise on status_code != 400: except HTTPException as exc: if exc.status_code != 400: raise; skipped += 1
evidence: Lines 103-108: except HTTPException: skipped += 1 — catches all HTTPExceptions including 500
backend/routes/backtest.py lines 934-937: run_backtest re-raises HTTPException and converts all other exceptions to HTTPException(500)

findings.title: QueryClient instantiated with no retry config
findings.why_it_matters: React Query default retry is 3 attempts with exponential backoff. With 5 polling hooks active simultaneously, a backend restart can generate up to 15 retry requests before all hooks settle to error state. Pre-migration code swallowed all errors with no retries. Negligible for single-user personal tool but a behavior change from the migration.
evidence: main.tsx line 8: const queryClient = new QueryClient() — no defaultOptions
React Query v5 default: retry: 3, retryDelay: exponential from 1s
5 hooks x 3 retries = up to 15 simultaneous retry requests on backend restart

residual_risks: C22 optimizer has no streaming or progress feedback — a 200-combo run that appears hung may prompt re-submission, doubling server load.

adaptiveMs() reads broker health from React Query cache synchronously inside the refetchInterval callback; if the broker cache entry is stale or absent at callback time, the normal interval is used and an unhealthy broker is missed for one poll cycle.
The optimizer runs all 200 combos even if the first combo fails with a 500 (bad ticker, invalid date range in base request). All 200 combos will call _fetch() before returning skipped=200.
testing_gaps: No test for optimizer endpoint under a slow/hanging run_backtest — need to verify threadpool saturation behavior under concurrent optimizer submissions.
No test for optimizer partial-failure scenario: some combos succeed, some fail with 500 — verify skipped count and results list integrity.
No test verifying adaptiveMs() correctly reads broker health from cache when called from refetchInterval callback (not during render).
No integration test for client disconnect during optimizer run to confirm server-side resources are eventually freed.
